ontology.foodex2_description: Fish fingers / fish sticks — breaded and fried fish product
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for fish finger as a prepared dish; SNOMED CT coverage for composite fried fish products is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fish fingers in Singapore is approximately 6–8 pieces, weighing around 200 g, often served as a snack or side dish with dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish sticks/fingers, breaded, baked or fried (SR Legacy ID 15264)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for breaded fried fish sticks used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values from the input record (energy 275 kcal, protein 17.5 g, fat 19.0 g, carbohydrate 9.0 g, sodium 425 mg) are broadly consistent with USDA data for deep-fried breaded fish products and have been retained with adaptation. Spicy Indian-style seasoning increases sodium slightly above the USDA baseline; this is reflected in the retained sodium value of 425 mg/100 g. Singapore HPB Foodo does not carry a direct entry for this specific preparation; USDA SR Legacy is the closest approved-source equivalent.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian-style spicy fish fingers. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on the closest analogous entry: breaded fried fish products from the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 35–45 due to the protein-dominant composition moderating glucose absorption and the wheat flour coating contributing modest starch. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (38 × 18.0) / 100 = 6.84, rounded to 6.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 425 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 200 g serving delivers 850 mg sodium, representing 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit fish finger portions to no more than 100 g per occasion, request low-sodium seasoning blends, avoid high-sodium dipping sauces, and prefer oven-baked preparations to reduce both sodium and fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~38) and modest carbohydrate (9 g/100 g) are favourable, but deep-frying adds significant fat (19 g/100 g) and caloric density (275 kcal/100 g), which can impair insulin sensitivity over time.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume fish fingers in moderate portions (100–150 g) given the low GI and high protein content, but should avoid high-sugar dipping sauces and prefer baked versions to reduce fat-driven caloric excess and long-term insulin resistance risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 19 g/100 g with saturated fat 4 g/100 g and trans fat 0.25 g/100 g; sodium 425 mg/100 g; cholesterol 60 mg/100 g — collectively elevate cardiovascular risk with regular consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit deep-fried fish finger consumption to occasional servings, prioritise baked or grilled fish preparations, and avoid pairing with high-sodium or high-sugar condiments to manage combined lipid and blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation; sodium 425 mg/100 g may worsen RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried fish fingers due to AGE formation; oven-baked fish fingers using anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric and ginger are a preferred alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Finger is a popular fried fish snack consisting of boneless white fish fillets marinated in a spicy seasoning blend — typically including turmeric, chilli, ginger-garlic paste, and salt — coated with wheat flour and beaten egg, then deep-fried until crispy and golden brown. In Singapore, this preparation reflects Indian culinary influence and is commonly sold through Indian-Muslim and North Indian restaurant outlets on food delivery platforms. The exterior is crunchy and well-seasoned, while the interior remains moist and flaky. Nutritionally, each 100 g delivers approximately 275 kcal, 17.5 g protein, 19 g total fat (including 4 g saturated fat and 0.25 g trans fat), and only 9 g carbohydrate, giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 38. Sodium is elevated at 425 mg/100 g due to the spice coating, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The high protein content supports satiety and moderates postprandial glucose response, making it a moderate-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which is a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or chronic inflammatory conditions. A standard serving is approximately 6–8 pieces (200 g). Allergens include fish, wheat (gluten), and egg. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish fingers?
Is fish finger high in sodium?
What is the GI of fish fingers?
Can I eat fish fingers with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indian-style spicy fish fingers exists in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN tables. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for breaded fried fish sticks, which is the closest approved-source equivalent. Input values from the GrabFood record are broadly consistent with USDA data and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for breaded fried fish products. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Indian-style spicy deep-fried fish finger preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. The USDA entry for breaded fried fish sticks does not disaggregate monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids for this product category. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (17.5), fat_g (19.0), and carbohydrate_g (9.0) are all non-zero. Input ethnic_tags ('Biryani', 'Butter Chicken', 'Curry', 'Curry Village') appear to be GrabFood restaurant metadata and have been excluded from the food record as they do not describe the dish itself. GI value of 38 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this specific preparation has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2024.
